---
title: "Bonus Exercise: Spot the Leakage -- SOLUTIONS"
subtitle: Machine Learning with R in the Life Sciences
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: false
  html_document:
    toc: true
    theme: flatly
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

### B.1

**Version A contains data leakage.** When you call `scale()` on the entire dataset
before splitting, the mean and standard deviation used for normalization are computed
from ALL samples -- including those that will end up in the test set. This means the
training data has been influenced by information from the test set.

### B.2

`scale()` computes the column mean and standard deviation. In Version A, these
statistics include test set values. So every scaled training value is slightly shifted
by information from samples that should be unknown during training. In Version B,
the recipe computes the mean and SD from the training data only, and applies
those same statistics to normalize the test data. This is the correct approach.

### B.3

**Version A contains data leakage.** The t-tests for feature selection are run on the
entire training set BEFORE cross-validation. This means every CV fold's training
partition already benefits from having seen information from its validation partition
(through the feature selection step). The selected genes are biased toward being
good predictors on this specific dataset, inflating CV performance.

### B.4

When published, the paper would report a specific gene set and a performance
estimate. But that gene set was selected using information that will not be
available for new data. On a completely independent cohort, the selected genes
may not be informative, and the reported performance will not be reproducible.
This is a major contributor to the replication crisis in ML-based biology studies.

### B.5

The dataset contains **confounding variables** that are perfectly correlated with
the class label: `batch` (tumor = batch_1, normal = batch_2) and
`measurement_time` (tumor = morning, normal = afternoon). A model can achieve
near-perfect accuracy by simply learning "batch_1 = tumor" or "morning = tumor"
without learning anything about the actual gene expression patterns. On new data
collected without this batch structure, the model would fail completely.

### B.6

1. **Batch ID** (batch_1 vs batch_2) -- directly encodes the class label
2. **Measurement time** (morning vs afternoon) -- acts as a proxy for the label

Both are technical artifacts, not biological signals.
